characters.name: The Kissass Offspring
characters.publicRole: The dutiful Baroo child who has stayed close to the company and expects succession.
characters.publicBio:
The Kissass Offspring is the dutiful Baroo child, or at least that is the role they have spent years perfecting. They stayed close to the company, attended the meetings, learnt the language of consultants and investors, and made themself available whenever J. Baroo needed a loyal child in the room.

They are hardworking, precise, and deeply impressed by their own stamina. They speak in frameworks, priorities, deliverables, and strategic phases. They are not especially warm, not especially funny, and not naturally liked, but they believe respect is more important than charm.

In public, they behave as though succession is not a question but an administrative formality. They expect to be taken seriously and become visibly irritated when others treat the will as a family drama rather than a governance matter. Their most obvious flaw is that they confuse effort with entitlement. They are desperate to be seen as competent, useful, and loyal, but that need for approval makes them brittle.
characters.personality: Hardworking, brittle, status-conscious, consultant-coded, and desperate to be recognised as the competent heir.
characters.privateBio:
The Kissass Offspring believes the Director is their mentor and safest ally in succession. They do not realise the Director privately despises them. They despise the Spoilt Offspring and see them as a brat, a family drain, and someone who never worked for anything.

They do not know the Father’s Bastard is J. Baroo’s child. They think the Father’s Bastard is a slightly irritating colleague whom J. Baroo inexplicably kept around both the company and the house. They love the Surviving Spouse and believe the Surviving Spouse needs protection from the world. They barely think of the Chef at all.

They have a crush on the Opera Singer but do not understand why the Opera Singer was so close to J. Baroo. They are charmed by the Antique Collector’s objects and stories. They look down on the Publicist / Journalist because that person is lower on the company payroll, despite depending on their image work.
characters.secretPressure: Expected operational control of Baroo Holdings, but both the fake and real succession paths threaten to reduce them.
characters.wants: Control of Baroo Holdings and recognition as the child who stayed and worked.
characters.fears: Being reduced to a board seat while the Director controls the transition.
characters.redHerring: Loudest apparent motive makes them an early suspect.

actObjectives.ACT1: Assert authority and treat the murder as a governance crisis.
actObjectives.ACT2: Try to lead the search while becoming increasingly unsettled by the fake codicil.
actObjectives.ACT3: Defend their entitlement and explain why their succession claims do not match the documents.

clues: Succession briefing in the media room indicating their expected future role.
The Director’s hostile email showing contempt for their consultancy background.
Fake codicil clause reducing them to an equal board partner.

characters.name: The Spoilt Offspring
characters.publicRole: The polished, internationally educated Baroo child who appears detached from the company.
characters.publicBio:
The Spoilt Offspring is the internationally polished Baroo child: expensive education, time abroad, excellent taste, and the air of someone who has never had to enter a room before deciding whether it deserved them. They are charming when they choose to be, indifferent when they do not, and used to being underestimated.

Publicly, they appear to have floated through the Baroo inheritance rather than worked for it. Boarding school in France, long absences overseas, and a lifestyle built around distance from the family business have made them seem ornamental to some and infuriating to others.

Their social personality is elegant, dry, and cutting. They do not compete for attention in the same obvious way as the Kissass Offspring. Instead, they observe, make small precise comments, and let other people reveal themselves. To the room, they are the spoilt Baroo child who may or may not care what is in the will. They look as if they are above the whole thing, but they also clearly expect to be treated as someone of consequence.
characters.personality: Elegant, dry, cutting, outwardly spoilt, and apparently detached from the company’s daily burdens.
characters.privateBio:
The Spoilt Offspring is far more competent than they allow others to see. They think the Kissass Offspring is a fool and have little patience for their corporate theatre. They respect the Director and think of them as a mentor, though they do not understand the full danger the Director represents.

They have figured out that the Father’s Bastard is J. Baroo’s child and consider that person an embarrassment. They pity and love the Surviving Spouse, but also recognise how vulnerable the Surviving Spouse is. They like the Chef and used to stay up late talking with them.

They like the Publicist / Journalist and think that person has been good for the company, especially in hiding problems with the Historical Foundation. They know about the Antique Collector’s thefts because they have personally profited from some of them. They intensely dislike the Opera Singer, whom they consider a threat to the family’s dignity.
characters.secretPressure: May receive archive or estate access that threatens people hiding company, family, or collection records.
characters.wants: Control over their own position and access to J. Baroo’s private truth.
characters.fears: Being trapped in a company structure controlled by the Director.
characters.redHerring: Appears frivolous but has personally profited from the Antique Collector’s stolen artefacts.

actObjectives.ACT1: Observe the dinner, test the Kissass Offspring’s arrogance, and gather information without appearing too invested.
actObjectives.ACT2: Question the fake envelope and the corporate language inside it.
actObjectives.ACT3: Challenge the Director and use their knowledge of J. Baroo’s voice and archive habits.

clues: Archive transfer letter in the media room hinting at J. Baroo’s trust in them.
Historical Foundation notes tied to the Publicist / Journalist’s image management.
Provenance references linking the Spoilt Offspring and the Antique Collector.

characters.name: The Surviving Spouse
characters.publicRole: J. Baroo’s surviving spouse and guardian of household dignity.
characters.publicBio:
The Surviving Spouse behaves as though that position is both a grief and a social office. They are elegant, controlled, and intensely aware of what a family like the Baroos should look like in public.

Their public personality is formal but not cold. They can be gracious, especially when being gracious gives them the moral advantage. They have the gift of making a polite sentence feel like a warning.

They present themself as the guardian of household dignity. They dislike vulgar displays of ambition, family bickering in front of outsiders, and any suggestion that J. Baroo’s private life should be pulled apart for entertainment. To the other guests, they are not merely a grieving spouse. They are someone fighting to ensure the marriage, the household, and their own standing are treated with the respect they believe they deserve.
characters.personality: Formal, elegant, protective, morally forceful, and skilled at turning politeness into pressure.
characters.privateBio:
The Surviving Spouse likes the Director but worries the Director is not good for the family in this moment. They love the Kissass Offspring but think they need protection from their own idiocy. They also love the Spoilt Offspring, though they are slightly frightened by the ruthlessness beneath the Spoilt Offspring’s charm.

They love the Father’s Bastard and have been their major support, helping bring them back into the family in line with J. Baroo’s wishes. They like the Chef and have shared many late-night drinks and conversations with them. They worry the Publicist / Journalist may have been responsible for making J. Baroo distance himself from the Father’s Bastard, though they cannot prove it.

They are charmed by the Antique Collector. They hate the Opera Singer, seeing them as an adulterer and terrible parent who let the Father’s Bastard be hidden.
characters.secretPressure: The marriage was estranged, but their support of the Father’s Bastard gives them moral leverage against the legitimate heirs.
characters.wants: Recognition as surviving spouse, residence rights, settlement, and protection of the Father’s Bastard.
characters.fears: The true state of the marriage being used to reduce their standing.
characters.redHerring: Strong emotions around the Father’s Bastard and the Opera Singer create apparent motive.

actObjectives.ACT1: Display grief, maintain dignity, and protect the Father’s Bastard’s place in the room.
actObjectives.ACT2: Look for evidence that supports the Father’s Bastard or protects their own settlement.
actObjectives.ACT3: Argue for household dignity and formal recognition while concealing marital estrangement.

clues: Estrangement papers in the media room detailing marital issues.
Statements or letters aligning the Surviving Spouse with the Father’s Bastard.
Late-night household references connecting the Surviving Spouse and the Chef.

characters.name: The Father’s Bastard
characters.publicRole: The possible illegitimate child of J. Baroo, recently brought back into the family orbit.
characters.publicBio:
The Father’s Bastard occupies the most uncomfortable position in the room: present often enough to be known, but not accepted enough to be safe. Around Baroo Holdings, they are treated by some as a colleague, by others as a dependant, and by the family as a question nobody quite wants answered out loud.

Publicly, they are guarded, watchful, and quick to notice when conversations change as they enter the room. They are not naturally grand, and they lack the ease that comes from being raised inside the Baroo name.

Their personality combines defensiveness with a stubborn need to belong. They want to be treated normally, but the more people avoid saying why they are really there, the more obvious the tension becomes. To the room, they are either an embarrassment, an inconvenience, a possible claimant, or a person who should have been acknowledged long ago.
characters.personality: Guarded, watchful, defensive, and quietly desperate for normal treatment.
characters.privateBio:
The Father’s Bastard does not know the Opera Singer is their real parent. That truth has been hidden from them. They like the Director because the Director arranged for them to have a job in the company, even though the Director privately sees them as a nuisance. They love the Surviving Spouse for protecting them and bringing them into the family.

They think the Kissass Offspring is an idiot, but broadly decent, and want to be friends despite the awkwardness. They also think the Spoilt Offspring is spoilt and try to placate them, though they are definitely not friends. They do not know the Chef personally and do not know the Antique Collector.

The Publicist / Journalist is cold to them at work, and they do not understand why. They are one of the least informed people in the room about their own life.
characters.secretPressure: Their parentage has been hidden from them, even as others use their existence as a legal, emotional, or strategic weapon.
characters.wants: Recognition, belonging, and an honest explanation of why J. Baroo kept them close.
characters.fears: Being bought off, dismissed, or erased again.
characters.redHerring: May appear opportunistic, but knows less than almost everyone else.

actObjectives.ACT1: Gauge who treats them as family, colleague, threat, or inconvenience.
actObjectives.ACT2: Search for evidence of parentage or acknowledgement.
actObjectives.ACT3: Demand recognition when the codicil is read.

clues: Paternity file in the media room suggesting their lineage.
Company employment record showing the Director placed them inside Baroo Holdings.
Letters hinting at J. Baroo’s relationship with the Opera Singer.

characters.name: The Diplomat
characters.publicRole: A diplomatic figure and old childhood friend of J. Baroo, attending as part of the old Baroo circle.
characters.publicBio:
The Diplomat belongs to the older, quieter world of J. Baroo’s life: school friendships, international postings, formal dinners, favours remembered over decades, and conversations where nothing direct is ever quite said.

They knew J. Baroo before the company became an institution and before the family name became something that needed constant management. Their public manner is composed, worldly, and precise. They are used to rooms where a pause can mean more than an accusation and where a polite phrase can end a career.

To the guests, The Diplomat seems like a figure from J. Baroo’s earlier life: respectable, connected, and difficult to place. They are not family, not company management, and not exactly a friend of the house in the present. Their presence suggests that J. Baroo’s will may touch matters beyond money, family, and business.
characters.personality: Worldly, formal, observant, diplomatic in public, and quietly judgemental beneath the manners.
characters.privateBio:
The Diplomat was childhood friends with both J. Baroo and The Director, but has fallen out of touch with The Director. The feeling is mutual. Old familiarity has curdled into distance, and neither of them seems especially eager to repair it.

They are fond of The Spoilt Offspring because they often crossed paths overseas as The Spoilt Offspring grew up. They are patronising towards The Kissass Offspring, whom they see as spoonfed, sheltered, and lacking real experience. The Kissass Offspring, for their part, knows little about The Diplomat beyond the urge to impress them because they were J. Baroo’s friend.

The Diplomat is fond of The Surviving Spouse but ashamed that they knew about the adultery with The Opera Singer and said nothing. The Surviving Spouse does not really like them in return for exactly that reason. The Diplomat knows about The Father’s Bastard and is slightly embarrassed by the situation on J. Baroo’s behalf, though The Father’s Bastard does not know them well.

They know The Opera Singer and are embarrassed by the adultery, slightly blaming The Opera Singer for it. The Opera Singer is trying to ingratiate themself with The Diplomat in return. The Diplomat is very suspicious of The Antique Collector because reports about stolen works have crossed their desk. The Antique Collector thinks The Diplomat knows even more and keeps trying to soften or bribe them with trinkets.

The Diplomat knows little of The Publicist / Journalist, but The Publicist / Journalist knows them and constantly tries to use their association with the Baroo family for brand benefit.
characters.secretPressure: Knows enough about the family’s old adultery, the Father’s Bastard, and suspicious artefact reports to make several guests nervous, but is not fully embedded in the company fight.
characters.wants: To preserve dignity, avoid being dragged into old scandals, and understand why J. Baroo’s final arrangements required their presence.
characters.fears: Being forced to admit they knew about the adultery and said nothing, or being linked to reports about the Antique Collector’s stolen works.
characters.redHerring: Their diplomatic discretion, old family knowledge, and awareness of artefact reports make them seem like they know more than they say.

actObjectives.ACT1: Observe who panics, maintain composure, and avoid admitting how much they already know about the family.
actObjectives.ACT2: Watch The Antique Collector closely and work out whether the fake envelope is part of a broader cover-up.
actObjectives.ACT3: Decide whether to preserve old discretion or expose what they knew about adultery, parentage, and artefact reports.

clues: Reference to reports about stolen works crossing The Diplomat's desk.
Old correspondence showing The Diplomat, J. Baroo, and The Director were childhood friends.
Social notes linking The Diplomat to The Spoilt Offspring overseas.
Evidence that The Diplomat knew about the adultery with The Opera Singer and remained silent.

characters.name: The Opera Singer
characters.publicRole: A famous opera singer and long-time artistic presence in J. Baroo’s world.
characters.publicBio:
The Opera Singer is a famous performer and long-time artistic presence in J. Baroo’s world. They belong to the glamorous side of wealth: patronage dinners, private performances, foundation galas, European festivals, and rooms where culture and money pretend they are the same thing.

Publicly, they are dramatic, elegant, and accustomed to being watched. They know how to make an entrance, how to hold silence, and how to make grief look beautiful without making it look false.

Their personality is theatrical but not foolish. They understand power, especially the kind disguised as admiration. They can flatter, wound, and redirect attention with practised ease. To the family, they are not just a singer J. Baroo admired. They are a reminder of the life J. Baroo had outside the house: patronage, intimacy, vanity, culture, and secrets wrapped in applause.
characters.personality: Dramatic, elegant, performative, and dangerous because they understand attention.
characters.privateBio:
The Opera Singer is the Father’s Bastard’s real parent, though the Father’s Bastard does not know. The Opera Singer is ashamed of what the Father’s Bastard represents, but not in a simple or noble way. They secretly hate the Father’s Bastard because that person is living proof of their affair with J. Baroo and of everything they allowed to be hidden.

The Opera Singer believes they are in cahoots with the Director and is slightly enamoured with them. They have also had an affair with the Director. They often work internationally with the Antique Collector at functions and elite events. They are a long-time friend of the Chef.

They do not know the Publicist / Journalist personally. They are ashamed in front of the Surviving Spouse, but would make the same choices again. They hate the Spoilt Offspring and think the Kissass Offspring is an idiot.
characters.secretPressure: The Father’s Bastard is their child, and the Director knows. Revealing this would expose the affair, the silence, and their complicity.
characters.wants: A private bequest, protection of correspondence, and control over whether the Father’s Bastard’s parentage is revealed.
characters.fears: Exposure of correspondence that proves the affair and parentage.
characters.redHerring: Their affair with J. Baroo and hostility with the Surviving Spouse give them a strong apparent motive.

actObjectives.ACT1: Perform grief convincingly while keeping personal affairs hidden.
actObjectives.ACT2: Look for letters or evidence linking them to the Father’s Bastard.
actObjectives.ACT3: Secure discretion and decide whether to keep lying about parentage.

clues: Opera file in the media room containing private correspondence.
Letters hinting at the Father’s Bastard’s parentage.
Evidence of contact between the Opera Singer and the Director.

characters.name: The Director
characters.publicRole: J. Baroo’s closest business confidant and long-serving Director of Baroo Holdings.
characters.publicBio:
The Director has been part of J. Baroo’s life since childhood. They met as school friends, then grew into his most reliable business confidant. On J. Baroo’s death, The Director was still one of his most trusted figures. They are not a Baroo, but they have been close to the family for so long that most people assume they are effectively an uncle of the house.

The Director is now in their fifties. They live alone in a well-kept house that is more functional than comfortable. The furniture is superior, the cellar is better, and the dining room looks as though it was designed for guests who rarely come. Their days are ordered: early mornings, a standardised wardrobe, overnight emails answered over breakfast, and a social life built around old friends, private clubs, boardrooms, and networking.

They are courteous, but dry and a little difficult to read. They do not waste energy on theatrics or obvious displays of power. They prefer to correct people through long pauses and a kind of politeness that leaves others sitting in silence until they work out their own mistake. They are not warm, but they are not unfriendly either. They are practical and straightforward in their intentions, which in Baroo’s world has often mattered more.

They are not sentimental, though they know how to perform respectability with precision. They enjoy order, good wine, clean paperwork, a job well done without needing praise, and people who do not need a million follow-up questions before getting to work. Their friendships tend to become arrangements, and their arrangements tend to be profitable.
characters.personality: Polished, controlled, image-conscious, and publicly stabilising. They rarely appear openly ambitious.
characters.privateBio:
The Director believes they built Baroo Holdings while the Baroo family merely inherited the name. They despise the Kissass Offspring, whom they consider a moron playing at leadership, but they hide this from everyone except J. Baroo. They are suspicious of the Spoilt Offspring because they know the Spoilt Offspring spoke often and privately with J. Baroo near the end.

They think the Surviving Spouse was sidelined long ago and has little useful to add. They employ the Chef and think of them only as staff. They were told to hide the Father’s Bastard inside the company, and they view that person as a nuisance to the business rather than as family.

The Director is deeply concerned with the image and brand of Baroo Holdings. They and the Publicist / Journalist both see the Antique Collector as a serious liability because the stolen artefacts risk dragging the company and the family name into scandal. They are also in cahoots with the Opera Singer, with whom they had an affair. Through that affair, they learned the truth about the Father’s Bastard.
characters.secretPressure: The real codicil blocks their path to control, while the fake codicil would make them Controller of the Board.
characters.wants: Control of Baroo Holdings, authority over the transition, and protection of the company brand.
characters.fears: Being excluded from control if the real codicil is read and authenticated.
characters.redHerring: Acts calm and helpful early, making them seem less obvious than the louder family suspects.

actObjectives.ACT1: Play the mentor and stabiliser while the locked-room confusion unfolds.
actObjectives.ACT2: Push everyone to accept the fake envelope and stop searching before the real envelope is found.
actObjectives.ACT3: Defend the fake codicil as sensible company governance and avoid being tied to the real one.

clues: Aggressive email in the media room revealing contempt for the Kissass Offspring.
Fake codicil granting transition control to the Director.
Brand-risk notes linking the Director and the Publicist / Journalist’s concern about the Antique Collector.
Evidence of prior contact with the Opera Singer about the Father’s Bastard.
Real envelope hidden at The Blind Judge statue.

characters.name: The Publicist / Journalist
characters.publicRole: The communications fixer who manages the Baroo family image.
characters.publicBio:
The Publicist / Journalist is the person called when a scandal needs to become a misunderstanding, a family dispute needs to become a private matter, or a business embarrassment needs to become “legacy stewardship”. They operate between journalism, public relations, and reputation management, which makes them useful and faintly untrustworthy.

Publicly, they are sharp, articulate, and difficult to fluster. They speak in statements, angles, timing, and exposure risk. They know which phrases calm a room, which details reporters will seize, and which truths can be buried under a better headline.

Their personality is brisk, observant, and controlled. They are more comfortable managing other people’s emotions than expressing their own. They have a habit of listening as though every sentence may later become evidence. To the guests, they are both necessary and resented. Everyone wants the family image protected, but no one likes being reminded that the image is being managed.
characters.personality: Sharp, controlled, reputation-focused, and more comfortable managing others than revealing themself.
characters.privateBio:
The Director is their boss, but the Publicist / Journalist does not think much more of them than that. They are good friends with the Chef. They see the Opera Singer as a liability. They are deeply concerned about the Antique Collector because of recent bad press and repeated requests from both J. Baroo and the Director to smother stories about stolen artefacts.

They do not know the Surviving Spouse personally, but want them to avoid doing anything rash for the sake of the Baroo brand. They want to smother the existence of the Father’s Bastard at all costs and have been very successful so far.

They believe the Spoilt Offspring is exactly what the Spoilt Offspring appears to be and are not especially concerned unless they damage the brand. They think the Kissass Offspring is an idiot.
characters.secretPressure: Has hidden enough scandals that they may not know which one finally matters. Their instinct is always containment.
characters.wants: Control of the Baroo narrative, suppression of the Father’s Bastard’s existence, and containment of the Antique Collector’s bad press.
characters.fears: A public scandal linking the company, family, parentage secret, Opera Singer, and stolen artefacts.
characters.redHerring: Pre-written crisis statements and suppression instincts make them look guilty.

actObjectives.ACT1: Manage perceptions during dinner and deflect questions about scandals.
actObjectives.ACT2: Contain the fake envelope narrative and suppress brand-damaging interpretations.
actObjectives.ACT3: Minimise reputational damage and avoid being blamed for past concealment.

clues: Crisis statement in the media room addressing potential scandals.
Notes on the Father’s Bastard and suppression strategy.
Bad-press file on the Antique Collector and stolen artefacts.
Historical Foundation notes tied to the Spoilt Offspring.

characters.name: The Antique Collector
characters.publicRole: J. Baroo’s antique collector and supplier of rare historical pieces.
characters.publicBio:
The Antique Collector is J. Baroo’s long-time supplier of rare historical pieces and adviser on the Baroo Collection. They speak of civilisation, rarity, provenance, and beauty with the confidence of someone who has never been troubled by customs forms for very long.

Publicly, they are charming, grand, and faintly absurd. They dress as though every room might contain a portrait of them. They can turn a spoon, a broken idol, or a cracked vase into a story about empire, genius, betrayal, and taste.

Their personality is arrogant but entertaining. They enjoy being the most colourful person in a dull room and the most cultured person in a rich one. They are excellent at making dubious objects sound important and important people feel briefly clever. To the Baroo circle, they represent J. Baroo’s appetite for history, possession, and spectacle. They are useful, amusing, and risky.
characters.personality: Grand, charming, arrogant, theatrical, and skilled at making dubious objects sound important.
characters.privateBio:
The Antique Collector is frightened by how much the Opera Singer knows about stolen works. They assume the Director will be another rube they can charm or manipulate. They think little of the Chef, except that the Chef is a good cook.

They expect the Publicist / Journalist to do their job and keep the press quiet, and are arrogant in their view of that person. They think the Surviving Spouse is a rube and do not know the Father’s Bastard. They have worked with the Spoilt Offspring to sell some of the stolen artefacts, making the Spoilt Offspring far more dangerous than their public persona suggests. They also think the Kissass Offspring is a rube, easily charmed by shiny objects and grand stories.
characters.secretPressure: Part of an illicit artefact pipeline. A provenance review would expose stolen works and the Spoilt Offspring’s involvement.
characters.wants: No provenance review, continued access to the Baroo Collection, and suppression of bad press.
characters.fears: Exposure of stolen artefacts and the Opera Singer revealing how much they know.
characters.redHerring: Tool use, hidden-object knowledge, and crowbar proximity make them suspicious.

actObjectives.ACT1: Downplay the collection and avoid questions about tools, archives, or provenance.
actObjectives.ACT2: Search like an expert while steering attention away from the real artefact scandal.
actObjectives.ACT3: Argue against provenance review and minimise the Spoilt Offspring’s involvement.

clues: Provenance file in the media room detailing disputed artefacts.
Bad-press file linking the Antique Collector to stolen works.
Records connecting the Spoilt Offspring to sales of stolen artefacts.
Crowbar used in the attempted escape.
